blocks.content: Surveying the dining hall of my residence, there is a sight that often unnerves me. People scattered across the large expanse of chairs and tables, each person secluded to their own area. Although I spot the sporadic table with a couple of people congregated together in conversation, the vast majority of them are by themselves. They all share the same posture. Heads bent, lost in the glow of an eerily uniform rectangle placed right beside their plates, or cradled between their hands.

I remember quite proudly telling an old high school teacher of mine how I managed to find a group of people that I consistently got food with, to which she replied, “wasn’t it always that way?” Because back when she had been in university, that was the norm. You would always got food with your friends. What I thought was so special and extraordinary that I would go as far as mentioning in a conversation was so blatantly ordinary that it didn’t even cross her mind that it was something that could change.
For the extroverted, this might seem like an out-of-touch story.
But I would posit that even they would agree that the person I am about to talk about is truly an amazing anomaly: my younger cousin.

Amidst the mind-numbing usage of electronics around me (and me included), exacerbated by the most intense amount of gaming that I had ever seen when I went back to China, I found solace in her presence.

*“As someone who has been trying to minimize screen time for many years, seeing her gave me hope. Those around me all have phones. It’s an expectation to always carry one around. But for the first time in a while, I witnessed someone who truly was off of their phones, and any electronics for that matter. It really was a breath of fresh air. She was immensely confident in herself. Happy and energetic. Unencumbered by the invisible shadow that burdened all phone users.”*

I said invisible because up until that day, I didn’t even realize how tangibly different someone who had not been poisoned by electronics could be. What I admired most was her unfathomably quick and natural sense of creativity. While I had trouble even thinking of conversation topics when talking to friends, she led my brother and me through a flurry of activities she seemingly thought of on the spot, jumping from playing cards and filling up water balloons to folding paper nunchucks and playing Chinese-English Pictionary.

As we finished the day off playing a heated match of Jackstraws using the plastic straw-like things that came with the water balloons (if you don’t know what I’m talking about, this is your sign to buy some water balloons), I realized the alternative she represented.

She was nearing the age where children started becoming more self-conscious about themselves. The transition towards that gradual fading of childhood innocence, which almost always resulted in some shift in who you are. However, from my interactions with her, I felt an unbreakable sureness in her sense of identity that I had never seen before. Someone who wasn’t naive about the harms of the world, yet carried herself in a way that was unapologetically herself, despite whatever came her way. I remember writing how happy I was to see a 7th-grade girl so fully comfortable in her own skin, her happiness purely intrinsic, coming fully from within herself.

On my birthday, she gifted me a tiny wooden box. Inside it was a jar of paper stars she meticulously folded. Looking at each star, I am filled with happiness and hope. But also, a pang of sadness as well.
Will we always remember how to fold paper stars?
